Metadata.Case_Number: Registrar's Appeal Nos 491 and 492 of 2010 (Originating Summons No 879 and 1197 of 2010)

Party_Details.Name: Hau Tau Thong

Facts.Text: Sometime in 2003, the relationship between Hau Tau Thong and his brother Hau Tau Khang began to deteriorate.
At all material times, Hau Tau Thong was the managing director overseeing the business operations of the Companies.
At all material times, Hau Tau Thong and Hau Tau Khang were co-signatories of all the Companies' bank accounts.
Sometime in 2003, the relationship between the brothers began to deteriorate.

In 2006, Hau Tau Thong and Hau Tau Khang entered into an agreement to keep the Sanur restaurants in operation with a view to selling the business and properties of Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and Sanur Holding Pte Ltd and to carry out an orderly winding up of the companies, referred to as the 2006 Agreement.
After the 2006 Agreement, each of the two brothers accused the other of unreasonable behaviour in managing the winding down process of the Sanur businesses.

The source states that the appellant was alleged to have failed to use reasonable diligence in filing the Companies' accounts for the financial year ending on 30 June 2008 and sending the said accounts for auditing, and that as a result the Companies incurred penalties from ACRA.

By 2009, all Sanur restaurants operated by Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and Sanur Holding Pte Ltd had ceased operations, while both Hau Tau Thong and Hau Tau Khang remained as directors of the companies.

The respondent claimed that while investigating into the affairs of the Companies, he was stonewalled by Ms Cecilia Tan, the Office Manager. The respondent then terminated the services of Ms Tan in September 2009 and took possession of the keys to the Companies' documentation cabinets.
Having had access to the Companies' accounts, the respondent apparently discovered that the cash deposits did not match the daily cash sales of the restaurants.
Having had access to the Companies' accounts, the respondent apparently discovered that the cash deposits did not match the daily cash sales of the restaurants.
In October 2009, the appellant and his wife started another Indonesian restaurant, Pepes, at the same location at Ngee Ann City.

Unknown to the appellant, the respondent then engaged the services of Stone Forest Corporate Advisory Pte Ltd to conduct a forensic examination of the accounts of the Companies so as to investigate the alleged cash discrepancies.

Thereafter, a report was issued by Stone Forest in which it was reported that "based on the monthly sales summary for all 3 restaurants, the total amount of cash sales not deposited into Sanur's bank accounts amounted to S$153,525.45".

In August 2010, the respondent applied for leave of court to commence a derivative action on behalf of the Companies against the appellant for alleged breaches of fiduciary duties pursuant to Originating Summons No 879 of 2010.

Daily cash deposit slips for SIRPL's outlet at Causeway Point for the period 1 July 2006 to March 2009.
Daily cash deposit slips for SIRPL's outlet at Centrepoint for the period 1 July 2006 to September 2009.

As a result, the Companies breached the Memorandum of Lease entered into with Frasers Centrepoint, which included a covenant binding SHPL to operate an Indonesian restaurant on the unit and to keep the restaurant open daily.

The source states that the appellant was alleged to have improperly managed the restaurant operations, such as the termination of the leases at Causeway Point and Suntec City and the ceasing of operations of the Centrepoint outlet, despite the fact that the business was still profitable.
The source alleges improper management of the restaurant operations, such as the termination of the leases at Causeway Point and Suntec City and the ceasing of operations of the Centrepoint outlet.

The source states that the appellant was alleged to have improperly managed the restaurant operations, including the ceasing of operations of the Centrepoint outlet, despite the fact that the business was still profitable.

The source states that the appellant was alleged to have failed to use reasonable diligence in filing the Companies' accounts for the financial year ending on 30 June 2008 and sending the said accounts for auditing, and that as a result the Companies incurred penalties from ACRA.

The source states that the appellant was alleged to have failed to use reasonable diligence in paying fees for services rendered by one M/s Spin Creative Pte Ltd, and consequently the latter instituted legal proceedings against the Companies and obtained judgment in default of appearance.

The source states that the appellant was alleged to have revoked the respondent's instruction to appoint a solicitor to defend the lawsuit commenced by Cecilia Tan against the Companies for wrongful dismissal.

The source states that the appellant was alleged to have revoked the respondent's instruction to appoint a solicitor to defend the lawsuit commenced by Cecilia Tan against the Companies for wrongful dismissal.
The source states that the respondent claimed the appellant deliberately allowed the offer for renewal of the lease for their flagship restaurant at Ngee Ann City to lapse.
The source states that the respondent claimed the appellant deliberately allowed the offer for renewal of the lease for their flagship restaurant at Ngee Ann City to lapse.

The source states that the appellant was alleged to have placed himself in a position of conflict against the interests of the Companies by incorporating Ochacha (Singapore) Pte Ltd and Simple Spice Pte Ltd, that ran the principal activities of retail sale of food and beverages and a restaurant business respectively.
The source states that the appellant was alleged to have failed to disclose the existence of these companies to the respondent.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director of Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and Sanur Holding Pte Ltd may exercise a statutory right to inspect the companies' accounting records and related documents in order to obtain full access to the companies' accounts for use in responding to a proposed derivative action concerning financial and operational conduct.
Party_Details.Rule: Under section 199(3) of the Companies Act, a company's accounting and other records kept at its registered office or other approved place are at all times open to inspection by its directors, and this right of inspection arises from the office of director and is generally exercisable without the director having to state reasons, limitations where the inspection is pursued for purposes unconnected with the performance of director functions or for purposes that would cause detriment to the company.
Party_Details.Application: Hau Tau Thong and Hau Tau Khang were co-directors and equal shareholders of Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and Sanur Holding Pte Ltd, with joint authority over the companies' bank accounts and access to their accounting records. From 2003 onward, their relationship deteriorated, and in 2006 they agreed to continue operating the Sanur restaurants while preparing to sell the business and properties and wind down the companies. By 2009, all Sanur outlets had ceased operations, but both brothers remained directors. During this period, the companies maintained accounting records, cheque book transaction records, and daily cash deposit slips for various outlets. In 2009, after terminating the office manager and obtaining the keys to the documentation cabinets, Hau Tau Khang accessed the companies' accounts, observed discrepancies between cash deposits and daily cash sales, and engaged Stone Forest Corporate Advisory Pte Ltd to conduct a forensic review, which produced a report quantifying cash sales not deposited into bank accounts. In August 2010, he applied for leave to commence a derivative action against Hau Tau Thong based on financial and operational conduct. In response, Hau Tau Thong sought to exercise his director's right under section 199(3) to inspect the full set of accounting records and related documents, including cheque book transaction records and daily cash deposit slips, rather than relying only on the subset of documents already selected and used in the forensic report. The requested inspection concerned company-owned records created during the period when he was managing director and co-signatory of the bank accounts, and the inspection would provide him with information about the companies' financial position and transactions his ongoing role as director and to the management of the companies' remaining affairs.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge analysed section 199 of the Companies Act and prior authorities, concluding that the director's right of inspection is conferred by statute in harmony with the common law and is described as absolute or unqualified in the sense that it is a legal incident of the office of director. The court noted that the right flows from the office and ceases when a person is no longer a director, that a director is not required to furnish reasons for inspection, and that in the absence of proof to the contrary the court assumes the right will be exercised for the benefit of the company. The judge accepted that external assistance such as accountants may be engaged to assist in inspection. The court then addressed the limitation that the right is lost where it is exercised for an ulterior purpose or to injure the company, citing George Wuu and related cases, and clarified that the exception is not confined only to situations where the company is injured but also extends to purposes unconnected with the discharge of director duties. The burden of proving improper purpose lies on the party opposing inspection, who must provide clear proof that the right would be abused and that substantial harm would be caused to the company. The judge rejected the respondent's argument that the right is confined to duties relating only to accounts or to present and future duties, holding instead that the right supports all director duties, including duties of care and diligence and the need to be informed about the company's affairs. The court considered McDougall and held that using inspection to obtain information to defend a claim by the company does not by itself establish an impermissible private purpose, because it can also serve the company's interest in resolving disputes on proper grounds. The judge found that in this case, allowing inspection could avoid unnecessary litigation costs if the accounts showed that the alleged financial irregularities did not occur. The court distinguished Oxford Legal on several grounds: in Oxford Legal, the director entity was effectively acting to protect a shareholder's position in a share valuation dispute rather than to discharge director obligations, and the inspection sought documents that would not have been obtainable under the discovery regime due to relevance and necessity thresholds. In contrast, here both brothers were current directors with equal rights to the companies' documents, and the appellant would in any event be entitled to the accounts at the discovery stage of the derivative action. The judge held that the appellant was not attempting to obtain documents that would otherwise be undiscoverable, but rather to access the full set of accounting records earlier than general discovery. The court concluded that the inspection had a sufficient nexus to the appellant's director duties and that there was no proven intention to use the information to injure the companies or for a purpose wholly unconnected with director functions. Accordingly, the appeal in Registrar's Appeal No 492 was allowed and the director's right to inspect the companies' accounts was enforced. The court then briefly addressed specific discovery under Order 24, holding that the application in Registrar's Appeal No 491 was premature because specific discovery before general discovery requires exceptional circumstances, which were not present, particularly given that the derivative action involved multiple categories of alleged misconduct beyond financial irregularities that would not be resolved solely by early discovery of the accounts. The court therefore dismissed RA 491, ordered a net costs payment of $3,500 by the respondent to the appellant for both appeals, and recorded appreciation for counsel's submissions.

Metadata.Case_Number: Registrar's Appeal Nos 491 and 492 of 2010 (Originating Summons No 879 and 1197 of 2010)

Party_Details.Name: Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and another (Sanur Holding Pte Ltd)

Facts.Text: By 2003, Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and Sanur Holding Pte Ltd were companies engaged in operating a chain of restaurants serving Indonesian cuisine under the trade name 'Sanur'.
By 2003, Hau Tau Thong and Hau Tau Khang were co-directors and equal shareholders of Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and Sanur Holding Pte Ltd.

In 2006, Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and Sanur Holding Pte Ltd continued operating their restaurants under the 2006 Agreement between the two director-shareholders, with the objective of selling the business and properties and carrying out an orderly winding up.

For the financial year ending 30 June 2008, Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and Sanur Holding Pte Ltd did not file their accounts and did not send them for auditing within the usual statutory timeframe, leading to penalties imposed by ACRA.

By 2008, Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and Sanur Holding Pte Ltd had not paid fees owed to Spin Creative Pte Ltd for services rendered, resulting in Spin Creative Pte Ltd commencing legal proceedings and obtaining a default judgment against the companies.
By 2009, all Sanur restaurants operated by Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and Sanur Holding Pte Ltd had ceased operations.
In September 2009, Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and Sanur Holding Pte Ltd terminated the employment of office manager Cecilia Tan.

In September 2009, the keys to the documentation cabinets containing the accounting and corporate records of Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and Sanur Holding Pte Ltd came into the possession of director Hau Tau Khang.

After gaining access to the documentation cabinets in September 2009, the accounting records of Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd showed cash deposits that did not match the daily cash sales figures for the restaurants.

Around late 2009, Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and Sanur Holding Pte Ltd were the subject of a forensic examination of their accounts by Stone Forest Corporate Advisory Pte Ltd, engaged by director Hau Tau Khang.

Stone Forest Corporate Advisory Pte Ltd issued a report stating that, based on monthly sales summaries for three Sanur restaurants, cash sales totalling S$153,525.45 were not deposited into Sanur's bank accounts.
From 1 July 2006 until the end of November 2007, Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd maintained transaction records for its DBS cheque book.
From 1 July 2006 to March 2009, Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd maintained daily cash deposit slips for its Causeway Point outlet.
From 1 July 2006 to September 2009, Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd maintained daily cash deposit slips for its Centrepoint outlet.

By 2008, the leases for Sanur restaurants at Causeway Point and Suntec City had been terminated and the Centrepoint outlet had ceased operations, although the Centrepoint business had previously been profitable.

By 2008, Sanur Holding Pte Ltd had entered into a Memorandum of Lease with Frasers Centrepoint that included a covenant to operate an Indonesian restaurant at a specified unit and to keep the restaurant open daily, and the cessation of operations at that unit resulted in non-compliance with that covenant.

In August 2010, Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and Sanur Holding Pte Ltd were the subject of an application in Originating Summons No 879 of 2010 by director Hau Tau Khang seeking leave to commence a derivative action on their behalf against director Hau Tau Thong.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and Sanur Holding Pte Ltd, through their director Hau Tau Khang, could resist a co-director's use of the statutory inspection right to obtain full access to the companies' accounting records when that co-director intended to use the information in defending a proposed derivative action concerning the companies' financial and operational affairs.
Party_Details.Rule: A company director's statutory right under section 199(3) of the Companies Act to inspect the company's accounting and other records is generally enforceable without the director having to justify the request, and the company may resist enforcement only by proving with clear evidence that the inspection is sought for an improper purpose, such as a purpose unconnected with the performance of director functions or a purpose that would cause detriment to the company, in which case the right is displaced.
Party_Details.Application: Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and Sanur Holding Pte Ltd operated Indonesian restaurants under the 'Sanur' trade name and maintained accounting records, cheque book transaction records, and daily cash deposit slips for their outlets. The companies experienced operational changes, including termination of leases and closure of outlets, and incurred penalties and litigation costs arising from late filing of accounts, unpaid service fees, and employment disputes. In 2009, director Hau Tau Khang obtained access to the companies' documentation cabinets, reviewed the accounts, and observed discrepancies between cash deposits and daily cash sales. He engaged Stone Forest Corporate Advisory Pte Ltd to conduct a forensic review, which produced a report quantifying cash sales not deposited into bank accounts. In August 2010, he applied for leave to commence a derivative action on behalf of the companies against co-director Hau Tau Thong, relying on financial and operational conduct. In response, Hau Tau Thong sought to exercise his statutory right as director to inspect the full set of the companies' accounting records and related documents, including those underlying the Stone Forest report. The companies, through counsel, argued that this inspection would be used to obtain 'ammunition' for his defence in the derivative action and to bypass the ordinary discovery process, and that such use was not connected to the performance of director duties. The court examined the companies' position in light of the statutory framework and case law, including authorities on improper purpose and detriment to the company, and considered whether the companies had provided clear proof that the requested inspection would be used solely for a private purpose or would harm the companies' interests. The court also considered that both directors had equal status and that the documents were company records that would in any event be disclosure at a later stage of the derivative proceedings.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge considered the companies' opposition to inspection, which was based on the argument that the co-director sought access to the accounts solely to defend himself in a derivative action and to circumvent the discovery process. The court reviewed authorities including George Wuu, Edman v Ross, Berlei, Law Wai Duen, McDougall, Barrett, and Oxford Legal. It held that the right of inspection is a legal incident of the office of director and is generally enforceable unless the company can show that the director is acting for an improper purpose or to injure the company. The court rejected a narrow interpretation that would confine the right to duties relating only to accounts or to present and future duties, and instead held that the right supports all director duties, including the need to be informed about the company's affairs. The judge accepted that the exception to the right is not limited to cases of actual injury to the company but also covers purposes unconnected with director functions. However, the burden of proof lies on the company to show clear proof of improper purpose and likely substantial harm. The court distinguished Oxford Legal, where the director entity was effectively acting to protect a shareholder's position in a share valuation dispute and sought documents that would not have been obtainable under the discovery regime. In the present case, both directors remained in office, the documents were company records that would be discoverable in due course, and the inspection was linked to the director's need to understand the accounts in the context of allegations about his management of the companies. The judge found that the companies had not demonstrated that the inspection would be used to injure them or for a purpose wholly unconnected with director duties, and that it was in the companies' interest to clarify the financial position and avoid unnecessary litigation if the accounts did not support the allegations. Accordingly, the court enforced the director's right of inspection and allowed the appeal in RA 492. On the separate issue of specific discovery in RA 491, the court applied Order 24 of the Rules of Court and authorities such as RHM Foods and Bank of India v Gobindram, holding that specific discovery before general discovery is exceptional and that the companies were entitled to resist early unilateral discovery where pleadings were not yet closed and the derivative action involved multiple categories of alleged misconduct beyond financial irregularities. The court therefore dismissed RA 491 while allowing RA 492 and ordered a net costs payment in favour of the appellant director.

Metadata.Case_Number: Registrar's Appeal Nos 491 and 492 of 2010 (Originating Summons No 879 and 1197 of 2010)

Party_Details.Name: Hau Tau Khang

Facts.Text: Sometime in 2003, the relationship between the brothers began to deteriorate.

The appellant and the respondent were co-directors with equal shareholdings in Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and Sanur Holding Pte Ltd, and were co-signatories of all the Companies' bank accounts.
Sometime in 2003, the relationship between the brothers began to deteriorate.

In 2006, Hau Tau Khang and Hau Tau Thong agreed to keep the Sanur restaurants in operation with a view to selling the business and properties of the companies and to carry out an orderly winding up, referred to as the 2006 Agreement.
After the 2006 Agreement, Hau Tau Khang and Hau Tau Thong each accused the other of unreasonable behaviour in managing the winding down process of the Sanur businesses.
By 2009, all Sanur restaurants had ceased operations, but Hau Tau Khang remained a director of Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and Sanur Holding Pte Ltd.
The respondent then terminated the services of Ms Tan in September 2009 and took possession of the keys to the Companies’ documentation cabinets.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, in light of his observations of cash discrepancies, penalties, unpaid service fees, lease decisions, and other operational matters, Hau Tau Khang could rely on the companies' accounting records and related documents to support an application for leave to commence a derivative action on behalf of the companies against his co-director concerning the management of the companies' financial and operational affairs.
Party_Details.Rule: A shareholder-director seeking leave to commence a derivative action on behalf of a company must demonstrate a prima facie case based on the available information, and at the leave stage the court generally does not adjudicate disputed facts or require full discovery, but may consider whether the applicant is acting in good faith and in the interests of the company when relying on company records and external reports.
Party_Details.Application: As a co-director and equal shareholder of Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and Sanur Holding Pte Ltd, Hau Tau Khang had access to the companies' accounting records and observed that recorded cash deposits did not match daily cash sales. He engaged Stone Forest Corporate Advisory Pte Ltd to conduct a forensic review, which produced a report quantifying cash sales not deposited into bank accounts. He also noted that the companies incurred penalties from ACRA due to late filing of accounts, faced a default judgment from Spin Creative Pte Ltd due to unpaid service fees, and were involved in litigation with a former employee. Additionally, he observed that leases for several outlets were terminated, that the Centrepoint outlet ceased operations despite prior profitability, and that a new restaurant, Pepes, was opened at the former flagship location by his brother and sister-in-law. He further became aware of his brother's involvement in other food and beverage companies, Ochacha (Singapore) Pte Ltd and Simple Spice Pte Ltd, which had not been previously disclosed to him. Using these observations and the Stone Forest report, he applied in August 2010 for leave to commence a derivative action on behalf of the companies against his co-director, setting out his complaints in an affidavit dated 26 August 2010. At the leave stage, the court considered that it would not resolve factual disputes or require full discovery but would assess whether the information presented, including the accounting records and forensic report, was sufficient to establish a prima facie basis for the proposed action and whether he appeared to be acting in the interests of the companies.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment under analysis did not determine the outcome of the derivative action itself or the leave application in Originating Summons No 879 of 2010. Instead, it focused on interlocutory appeals concerning a co-director's right to inspect the companies' accounts and an application for specific discovery. The court noted that at the leave stage for a derivative action, it is not appropriate to adjudicate disputed issues of fact and that the test is whether a prima facie case has been made out, as set out in authorities such as Teo Gek Luang, Agus Irawan, and Pang Yong Hock. The judge recorded that the respondent director, Hau Tau Khang, relied on multiple grounds for the proposed derivative action, including financial irregularities suggested by the Stone Forest report, late filing of accounts and resulting penalties, unpaid service fees leading to default judgment, handling of employment litigation, lease decisions and closure of outlets, and the opening of a new restaurant and involvement in other food and beverage companies. The court considered these matters when assessing whether early specific discovery of the companies' accounts was necessary or desirable at the leave stage. It concluded that discovery of the accounts would not summarily address the non-financial allegations and that it was therefore difficult to see how early discovery would dispose of the intended derivative action at the leave stage. On that basis, the court agreed with the Assistant Registrar that the application for specific discovery was premature and dismissed Registrar's Appeal No 491. The judgment did not, however, decide whether leave to commence the derivative action would ultimately be granted or refused, leaving the substantive outcome of the derivative proceedings unresolved in this decision.
